title: CGPSC 2026 — Chhattisgarh State Services Exam
meta_description: CGPSC SSE 2026: Prelims Feb 22, Mains May 16–19. Deputy Collector, DSP. Tribal culture, Bastar, mining. 238+ vacancies.

hero.one_liner: Chhattisgarh's state civil services exam — Deputy Collector, DSP, Naib Tehsildar positions through CGPSC State Service Examination

what_is_it: CGPSC (Chhattisgarh Public Service Commission) State Service Examination (SSE) recruits for Group A and Group B administrative, police, and revenue posts in Chhattisgarh state. With 238+ vacancies announced for 2026, this is one of the larger state PSC recruitment drives. The exam follows the standard pattern: Preliminary (2 papers — GS and CSAT), Mains (multiple papers), and Interview. Top ranks lead to postings as Deputy Collectors, Deputy Superintendents of Police, Naib Tehsildars, and other district-level administrative officers. CGPSC is designed for candidates who want to serve Chhattisgarh's development and governance.

Chhattisgarh-specific content constitutes 25–30% of CGPSC questions and covers: tribal culture and history (Bastar region's unique tribal traditions — Dussehra festival, Madai ritual, tribal governance systems), Chhattisgarh's formation as a state (November 1, 2000, carved from Madhya Pradesh), mineral wealth (iron ore, coal, limestone, bauxite — CG is India's mineral storehouse), industrial heritage (Bhilai Steel Plant, NTPC Korba), forest and environmental policies, and Naxal-affected area development. The state's rice bowl status (producing 15% of India's rice) and agricultural economy are also important.

CGPSC has a significant advantage for local aspirants: lower competition compared to neighboring MPPSC (1–1.5 lakh applicants vs 4+ lakh for MP). While the question difficulty is comparable, the competition-to-vacancy ratio is more favorable. Additionally, Chhattisgarh's lower cost of living means government salaries (₹75,000–1,05,000/month for Deputy Collector) provide a comfortable lifestyle. For candidates from Chhattisgarh, especially those with tribal or rural background, CGPSC represents a pathway to serve their community while building a prestigious career in state administration.

key_details.label: Education Required
key_details.value: Bachelor's degree from recognized university (any stream)

key_details.label: Domicile Requirement
key_details.value: Most posts require CG domicile (check notification)

exam_pattern.description: Paper 1: GS (200 marks). Paper 2: CSAT (200 marks, qualifying). Duration: 2 hours each. Negative marking: 1/3rd per wrong.
GS-I, II, III, IV + Language + Optional. Interview follows Mains.

extra_sections.heading: Chhattisgarh State-Specific Topics
content:
Bastar Region & Tribal Culture

Bastar district (southern Chhattisgarh) is unique — home to Gond, Abhuj Maria, Dhurwa, and other tribal communities with distinct cultural practices.

Bastar Dussehra (September–October) is the most famous festival — unlike other Dussehra celebrations, Bastar Dussehra honors the forest deity instead of Ram's victory, reflecting tribal animistic beliefs.

The Madai ritual (biennial gathering of Muria Gond tribe for community decision-making) represents tribal governance systems. Chhattisgarh's 39.2% tribal population makes tribal culture central to state identity.
CGPSC expects knowledge of tribal art forms, marriage customs, agricultural practices (shifting cultivation in some areas), and forest-based livelihoods.

State Formation & Political History

Chhattisgarh was created on November 1, 2000, by bifurcating Madhya Pradesh. Before this, Chhattisgarh was known as 'Dakshin Kosala' in ancient texts and was part of various empires: Satavahana, Vakataka, Kalachuri, and finally British colonial rule.
Key figures in state formation: Atal Bihari Vajpayee (PM who approved bifurcation), Digvijay Singh (last CM of united MP). The first CM of Chhattisgarh was Ajit Jogi (2000–2003).
CGPSC asks historical questions about Chhattisgarh's ancient kingdoms, medieval rulers, colonial period governance, and modern state formation movement.

Mineral Resources & Mining Industry

Chhattisgarh is India's mineral treasure — produces 30% of India's iron ore, 10% of coal, 35% of limestone, 30% of tin, 40% of dolomite. Major mining regions: Dalli-Rajhara (iron ore — Chhattisgarh's richest), Korba (coal), Raigarh (coal), Rajnandgaon (limestone).
Mining contributes significantly to state revenue but creates environmental and social challenges: water pollution, dust emissions, displacement of tribal communities.

CGPSC emphasizes: (1) location and type of mines, (2) economic contribution, (3) environmental impact and mitigation measures, (4) rehabilitation of displaced persons under MMDR (Mines and Minerals Development and Regulation) Act.

Bhilai Steel Plant & Industrial Development

Bhilai Steel Plant (BSP), established in 1959 with Soviet technical assistance, is India's largest steel producer and a symbol of Chhattisgarh's industrial prowess. It's a major employment hub (15,000+ workers) and contributor to state economy.

NTPC Korba (coal-fired thermal plant) and Korba Aluminium Smelter are other major industries. These plants have made Chhattisgarh an industrial powerhouse but also created pollution in Korba and Raipur.
CGPSC questions link industrial development to: labor welfare, environmental responsibility, skill development, and state economic growth.

Rice Bowl of India — Agriculture & Food Security

Chhattisgarh produces 15% of India's rice despite having only 3% of cultivated land — this is achieved through high yields and agricultural technology adoption. Major crops: rice (Kharif and Rabi), soybean, cotton, sugarcane.

Chhattisgarh's agricultural success is due to: favorable climate (600–1,600 mm annual rainfall), plains and river basins (Mahanadi, Godavari, Narmada), government support (minimum support price, subsidized inputs), and Green Revolution adoption. The state has a food surplus position and exports rice nationally.
Ironically, despite producing so much food, parts of Chhattisgarh (especially tribal areas) face malnutrition — a socio-economic paradox CGPSC explores.

Naxal-Affected Areas & Development Challenges

Southern and eastern Chhattisgarh (Bastar, Sukma, Dantewada, Bijapur) are severely affected by Naxal insurgency. The underlying causes: tribal land dispossession, mining displacement, poverty, lack of development.

Government response: AAGAMAN scheme (rehabilitation of Naxal-surrendered cadres), village defense committees, development of road and telecom infrastructure, educational and health facilities in remote areas.
CGPSC expects officers to understand: root causes of Naxalism, counter-insurgency strategies, development as counter-Naxalism tool, tribal rights balance, and human rights in conflict zones.

preparation_tips: MPPSC and CGPSC share 70% syllabus — if preparing for MPPSC, add 15–20 hours of CG-specific content and appear for CGPSC as well. Focus on: Bastar culture, state formation, mineral resources, Bhilai Steel Plant, Chhattisgarh's rice production, and Naxal-affected area policies. This double-exam strategy maximizes your options.

Bastar Dussehra and tribal governance are must-know topics. Unlike mainland Dussehra (Ram–Ravana narrative), Bastar Dussehra is about forest worship and tribal rituals. Study 2–3 articles or documentary clips to understand the cultural significance — this knowledge helps in descriptive Mains answers and interview discussions.

Current affairs specific to Chhattisgarh: Follow Chhattisgarh Samachar (local news), government press releases on industrial projects, mining policies, and Naxal operations. National news channels sometimes miss state-level developments. Dedicate 30 minutes daily to CG current affairs from month 3 onwards.

Prelims has 100 GS questions in 120 minutes = 72 seconds per question. Time management is critical. Practice mocks at this speed. In CG-specific topics, you should aim for 80%+ accuracy since these are relatively straightforward — use this to gain marks advantage over all-India competitors who lack CG knowledge.

For Mains, practice writing on CG-specific case studies: 'Bhilai Steel Plant's role in Chhattisgarh's industrialization and environmental challenges', 'Bastar's tribal culture and development', 'Mining in Chhattisgarh and tribal displacement — balancing growth and rights'. These topics often appear as Mains questions and interview questions.

faqs.question: Is Chhattisgarh domicile required for CGPSC?
faqs.answer: Most CGPSC posts require Chhattisgarh domicile. However, the definition of domicile and relaxation for different categories vary per post. Check the official notification carefully — some open posts (especially in specialized departments) may be open to all-India candidates. If domicile is required, you typically need to have lived in CG for 5+ years or be a CG native.

faqs.question: CGPSC vs MPPSC — which is easier?
faqs.answer: CGPSC has lower competition (1–1.5 lakh applicants vs 4+ lakh for MPPSC), making it easier in terms of competition-to-vacancy ratio. Question difficulty is comparable. If you're from CG, CGPSC is your primary target. If on MP-CG border, prepare for both — MPPSC has more vacancies but also more competition.

faqs.question: What's the typical salary and posting after selection as Deputy Collector in CG?
faqs.answer: Starting salary: ₹75,000–1,05,000/month (including basic, DA, and allowances). Postings are in CG's 27 districts — some urban (Raipur, Durg, Bilaspur) and some remote (Bastar, Sukma). First posting is typically based on merit (top rankers get better district choices), but Naxal-affected areas may receive additional allowances. Cost of living in CG is low, so the salary provides a comfortable lifestyle.

faqs.question: Are there more vacancies in CGPSC 2026 than previous years?
faqs.answer: Yes. CGPSC 2026 announced 238+ vacancies, which is higher than 2024–2025 cycles (180–215 vacancies). This increase is due to CG government's focus on administrative capacity building. More vacancies = better odds for candidates. This is a good year to appear for CGPSC if eligible.

faqs.question: How to prepare for Naxal-affected area governance questions in CGPSC?
faqs.answer: Study: (1) root causes of Naxalism (tribal land issues, mining displacement, poverty), (2) government counter-measures (AAGAMAN scheme, Bastar Police model, village committees), (3) development initiatives (roads, schools, health centers in Bastar), (4) Naxal organizations' evolution, (5) human rights concerns in conflict zones. Read reports from Human Rights Watch and PSAC (People's Solidarity and Action Committee) for balanced perspectives. This knowledge helps in Mains essays and interview.

faqs.question: Can I take an optional subject that's not mainstream in CGPSC?
faqs.answer: CGPSC allows 20+ optional subjects including History, Geography, Public Administration, Law, Agriculture, Engineering, Medicine, etc. Choose a subject where you have strong academic background or subject expertise. Unlike UPSC, state PSCs are more flexible with optional subjects — some candidates even score well with unconventional choices if they have deep knowledge. Align your choice with your strength, not what others choose.

syllabus.subject: General Studies Paper I
syllabus.topics: Indian history, geography, polity, economics. Chhattisgarh-specific: CG history (Gond kingdom, tribal movements), CG geography (mineral resources — coal, iron ore, bauxite), CG tribal culture (Bastar Dussehra, Madai), CG government schemes (Mahtari Vandana)

syllabus.subject: CSAT / General Aptitude (Qualifying)
syllabus.topics: Comprehension, logical reasoning, analytical ability, basic numeracy, data interpretation, decision making

syllabus.subject: Chhattisgarh Specific GK
syllabus.topics: CG history (Gond kingdom, tribal movements), CG geography (mineral resources — coal, iron ore, bauxite), CG tribal culture (Bastar Dussehra, Madai), CG government schemes (Mahtari Vandana)

books.title: Chhattisgarh State GK
books.author: CG GK by local publishers, Lucent GK + CG special
books.subject: Chhattisgarh GK

study_plan.tasks: Read NCERTs for History, Geography, Polity. Start Chhattisgarh state GK book. Study state-specific history, geography, culture, and current schemes. Daily newspaper reading for current affairs.
Laxmikanth Polity, Ramesh Singh Economy. Deep dive into Chhattisgarh-specific topics — this is what separates State PSC from UPSC. Start answer writing practice for Mains (250 words).
Solve last 10 years CGPSC papers. Weekly full mocks. Focus on Chhattisgarh current affairs and state government policies. Interview preparation alongside.
